elements.sent-2411-001: Common hand-capturing methods include Keypoint-skeleton tracking, which is preferred for real-time, efficient motion capture but lacks surface information, and mesh-based capturing, which provides superior detail for physical interactions but is time-intensive and costly annotations.
elements.sent-2411-002: A unified approach is needed to integrate skeleton and mesh tracking into a standardized, high-fidelity representation.
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce UniHands, a novel method for creating personalized hand models from wild-collected keypoints.
elements.sent-2411-004: By leveraging popular parametric hand models, MANO~\cite{romero2022embodied and NIMBLE~\cite{li2022nimble , UniHands reconstructs personalized hands and offers a scalable, versatile solution that integrates seamlessly with 3D modeling and XR applications.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: Additionally, it derives unified hand joints from the reconstructed meshes for smoother integration into various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments on the FreiHAND~\cite{zimmermann2017learning and InterHand2.6M~\cite{moon2020interhand2 datasets show that our reconstructions achieve precise alignment between keypoints and mesh vertices ($PJ/PV < 0.1 mm$) when the data format matches our optimization models.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: A further pilot empirical study with nine participants further evaluated the hand reconstruction subjectively when the wild-collected joint data does not match our models.
elements.sent-2411-008: Results indicated high fidelity in gesture and motion representations, with participants significantly preferring our unified joint set over existing configurations ($p$-value 0.016).
elements.sent-2411-009: The overview of UniHands is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:teaser of two major components: hand mesh reconstruction from wild-collected keypoints and hand joints derivation from the reconstructed mesh.}
elements.sent-2411-010: We designed a coarse-to-fine optimization method to refine the pose and shape ($\theta, \beta$) parameters of the MANO~\cite{romero2022embodied hand model and its global wrist rotation ($R_w$) using gradient descent, based on input keypoints and derived joints from the reconstructed mesh. \begin{equation}E_{key} = \sum_{i}^{N} || \mathbf{k}_i - \mathbf{J}_i(\theta, \beta, R_w) || ^2\end{equation} where $\mathbf{k _i$ are the input keypoints ($i \in [1,...,N]$) and $\mathbf{J _i(\theta, \beta, R_w)$ are the derived joints from the hand mesh.}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Coarse Stage: Start with an initial hand pose $\Bar{\theta}$ and mean shape $\Bar{\beta}$, optimizing for wrist rotation $R_w$.
elements.sent-2411-012: Fine Stage: Use two optimizers: one to refine pose and shape parameters, and another to fine-tune $R_w$ using Adam.
elements.sent-2411-013: Besides $E_{key}$, we apply 3D mesh regularizations for integrity and smoothness. \begin{equation}E_{reg} + E_{smooth} = \| \mathbf{\beta} \|^2 + \| \mathbf{\theta} \|^2 + \sum_{i} \sum_{j \in N(i)} \| \mathbf{v}_i - \mathbf{v}_j \|^2\end{equation} where $\mathbf{v _i$ and $\mathbf{v _j$ are adjacent mesh vertices, and $N(i)$ denotes the set of neighboring vertices of vertex $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: The total alignment error adds up to: \begin{equation}E = E_{key} + \lambda_{reg} E_{reg} + \lambda_{smooth} E_{smooth}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-015: The MANO model identifies 16 hand joints but lacks complete anatomical accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-016: The NIMBLE model, developed from CT scans, offers better alignment but may misalign in new poses since it models bones and skin separately.
elements.sent-2411-017: We address this by aligning the meshes from both models and fusing their skeletons (Fig.~\ref{fig:fusion-joints (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-018: To effectively derive joints from MANO, we first aligned the MANO mesh with NIMBLE using one-time optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-019: Then, we trained a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to directly predict NIMBLE-like joints from the MANO mesh (Fig.~\ref{fig:fusion-joints (b)).}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/method.pdf} \caption{Method illustrations. (a) Our unified joints (middle) combines the strengths of both MANO (left) and NIMBLE (right) keypoints. (b) Our MLP derives NIMBLE joints from MANO mesh.} \label{fig:fusion-joints}
elements.sent-2411-020: Tab.~\ref{tab:comp presents our reconstruction evaluations on FreiHAND~\cite{zimmermann2017learning and InterHand2.6M~\cite{moon2020interhand2 datasets.}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: We calculated the mean position errors per joint and vertex (PJ/PV) and their standard deviations (PJ-std/PV-std) between the estimated and actual vertices/keypoints.
elements.sent-2411-022: The results demonstrate high accuracy ($PJ/PV < 0.1 mm$), indicating effective mesh reconstruction when keypoints derived from wild-collected 3D hand models are aligned with ours.
elements.figure-2411-002: \begin{tabular}{c|ccccccc} Dataset PJ PV PJ-std PV-std FreiHAND~\cite{zimmermann2017learning} 0.027 0.022 1.029 1.77 InterHand2.6M~\cite{moon2020interhand2} -0.0047 -0.012 1.29 2.46 \end{tabular} \caption{Hand reconstruction evaluation results on FreiHAND~\cite{zimmermann2017learning} and InterHand2.6M~\cite{moon2020interhand2} datasets in millimeters (mm).}\label{tab:comp}
elements.sent-2411-023: We conducted an empirical study to evaluate hand reconstructions when wild-collected joint data does not align with our optimized models to validate the practical applicability of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-024: Participants: Nine participants with varying expertise levels.
elements.sent-2411-025: Study Material: Nine reconstructed hands were randomly selected from three datasets (Shrec21, Shrec22, and Thunder01), each collected with different technologies (Ultraleap, HoloLens2, and Mediapipe).
elements.sent-2411-026: Each hand is represented with three sets of joints: MANO, NIMBLE, and our unified model.
elements.sent-2411-027: Task Design: Participants assessed the reconstructed hand meshes and derived joints in Blender scenes across three tasks: [T1] Realism: How realistic the derived hand joints are in the reconstructed mesh? [T2] Accuracy: How closely the joints match the raw gesture data? [T3] Naturalism: How natural the skin looks in the motions? Results: 81 data points were collected per participant (i.e., 3 tasks $\times$ 9 gestures $\times$ 3 joint sets).
elements.sent-2411-028: Participants rated each sample on a scale from 1 to 10, then selected the top-rated sample as the best (1) and rated the others as 0.
elements.sent-2411-029: Fig.~\ref{fig:study-res (a) presents both the raw scores and mapped ranks.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Box plots show similar ratings for the first two tasks across joint sets; however, the fused model significantly outperformed the others in the third task.
elements.sent-2411-031: The mapped results further demonstrate that the fused model excels in accuracy and naturalism in animation, with a statistically significant improvement($p$-value $=0.016, < 0.05$).
elements.sent-2411-032: Participants completed a post-study questionnaire combining a modified System Usability Scale (SUS) and Task Load Evaluation (TLE), spanning six dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-033: Results indicate that participants consistently rated UniHands highly for performance efficiency, realism, and usefulness, demonstrating strong practical endorsement for using the system in their own work.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/study-res2.pdf} \caption{Evaluation Results. (a) Evaluations of three tasks across three joint sets, showing raw values (left) and mapped ranks (right). (b) Results from the modified System Usability Scale (SUS)/Task Load Evaluation (TLE) are weighted and displayed on a spectrum: lower ratings appear on the left in warmer hues, while higher ratings are on the right in cooler hues.} \label{fig:study-res}
elements.sent-2411-034: We introduced UniHands, a novel pipeline that bridges the gap between low-fidelity hand motion tracking and high-fidelity demands in XR tasks.
elements.sent-2411-035: It offers personalized, parameterized hand models, making it an essential tool for animation, synthetic data generation, and training in hand-related tasks.
elements.sent-2411-036: Evaluations on public datasets demonstrated the high accuracy and robust performance on hand mesh reconstruction.
elements.sent-2411-037: An empirical study confirmed its superior handling of natural gestures and motions, with participants awarding high marks for realism, accuracy, and naturalism.
elements.sent-2411-038: Notably, our unified MANO-NIMBLE fusion joints were consistently rated as providing the most natural appearance in skeleton animations.
elements.sent-2411-039: Another key finding is that people are highly sensitive to subtle anomalies in hand motion, especially when dealing with high-fidelity data.
elements.sent-2411-040: Future efforts should aim to refine techniques that balance anatomical accuracy with the fidelity of wild-collected data, to ensure high-quality hand pose reconstructions.
elements.sent-2411-041: Additionally, further studies in XR environments could personalize and improve hand modeling experiences.. \acknowledgments{ \bibliographystyle{abbrv-doi.}}
